questions.question: Consider a machine with a byte addressable main memory of 2^32 bytes divided into blocks of size 32 bytes. Assume that a direct mapped cache having 512 cache lines is used with this machine. The size of the tag field in bits is _______.
questions.to_find: Number of bits in the tag field for a direct-mapped cache.

understand.plain: We have a 32-bit byte-addressable memory split into 32-byte blocks. The cache is direct-mapped with 512 lines. A physical address is split into tag, index (cache line), and block offset fields. We need to find how many bits are left for the tag after accounting for index and offset bits.

keywords.explain: Each memory block maps to exactly one cache line determined by the index field of the address.
keywords.example: A memory with 8 cache lines uses 3 index bits so address bits 3-5 select the line.

keywords.explain: The upper bits of a physical address used to distinguish which memory block currently occupies a cache line.
keywords.example: If address = 20 bits, index = 4, offset = 3, then tag = 20 - 4 - 3 = 13 bits.

keywords.explain: The lower bits of an address that identify a specific byte within a cache block.
keywords.example: A 32-byte block requires log_2(32) = 5 offset bits.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='120'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='30' width='260' height='50' class='hi'/><rect x='270' y='30' width='130' height='50' class='bg'/><rect x='400' y='30' width='110' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='140' y='60' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Tag (18 bits)</text><text x='335' y='60' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Index (9 bits)</text><text x='455' y='60' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Offset (5 bits)</text><text x='260' y='20' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>32-bit Physical Address</text><text x='10' y='105' class='sub mut'>bit 31</text><text x='400' y='105' class='sub mut'>bit 14</text><text x='500' y='105' class='sub mut'>bit 0</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 32-bit address split into 18-bit tag, 9-bit index (512 cache lines), and 5-bit block offset (32-byte block).

given.aim: Find the number of tag bits for a direct-mapped cache with the given memory and cache parameters.

terms.example: Each unique byte location needs a 32-bit address.

terms.meaning: Cache has 512 = 2^9 lines, each addressed by 9 index bits.
terms.example: Memory address modulo 512 gives the cache line index.

given.plan: Physical address = tag bits + index bits + offset bits. Rearranging: tag bits = 32 - index bits - offset bits = 32 - 9 - 5 = 18.

steps.title: Calculate offset bits
steps.formula_raw: offset bits = log_2(block size in bytes)
steps.apply: Block size = 32 bytes = 2^5 bytes. So offset bits = 5.
steps.note: These bits identify the byte position within a block.

steps.title: Calculate index bits
steps.formula_raw: index bits = log_2(number of cache lines)
steps.note: In direct-mapped cache, index directly selects the cache line.

steps.title: Calculate tag bits
steps.formula_raw: tag bits = total address bits - index bits - offset bits
steps.note: Tag bits are the remaining most-significant bits used to verify if a block in the cache is the right one.

solution.result: The size of the tag field is 18 bits.

questions.formula_note: Address partitioning for direct-mapped cache: tag bits = total_address_bits - log2(cache_lines) - log2(block_size_bytes).

questions.question:
The size of the physical address space of a processor is 2^N bytes. The word length is 2^2 bytes. The capacity of cache memory is 2^M bytes. The size of each cache block is 2^4 words. For a K-way set-associative cache memory, the length (in number of bits) of the tag field is
A. N - M - 2
B. N - M + log_2(K)
C. N - M - 4 + log_2(K)
D. N - M - 2 + log_2(K)
questions.to_find: The formula for the number of tag bits in a K-way set-associative cache in terms of N, M, K.

understand.plain: This is a parametric cache question. The physical address space is 2^N bytes (so N-bit addresses). The cache has capacity 2^M bytes, block size 2^4 words (each word = 4 bytes = 2^2 bytes), and is K-way set-associative. We must derive a formula for tag bits.

keywords.explain: The cache is divided into sets, each holding K lines. An address is split into tag, set-index, and block-offset.
keywords.example: 4-way set-associative: each set has 4 lines; sets = total_lines / 4.

keywords.explain: Determined by block size in bytes. Block = 2^4 words x 2^2 bytes/word = 2^6 bytes => 6 offset bits.
keywords.example: Any block of 2^b bytes needs b offset bits to address every byte inside.

keywords.explain: Determined by the number of sets = (total_lines) / K = (2^M / 2^6) / K.
keywords.example: With 2^M cache bytes, 64-byte blocks, K-way: sets = 2^(M-6)/K => index = M-6-log_2(K).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='540' height='120'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='30' width='240' height='50' class='hi'/><rect x='250' y='30' width='170' height='50' class='bg'/><rect x='420' y='30' width='110' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='130' y='60' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Tag (N-M+log_2 K)</text><text x='335' y='60' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Index (M-6-log_2 K)</text><text x='475' y='60' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Offset (6 bits)</text><text x='270' y='20' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>N-bit Physical Address</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: N-bit address split into tag (N - M + log2(K) bits), set index (M - 6 - log2(K) bits), and block offset (6 bits for 64-byte blocks).

given.aim: Derive the formula for tag bits in a K-way set-associative cache with given address, cache, and block parameters.

terms.meaning: N-bit addresses are used to access any byte in memory.

terms.term: Block size = 2^4 words x 2^2 bytes = 2^6 bytes
terms.meaning: Each cache block holds 64 bytes, requiring 6 offset bits.
terms.example: Word = 4 bytes, 16 words per block = 64 bytes = 2^6.

given.plan: Compute offset bits from block size. Compute index bits from number of sets. Tag = N - index - offset.

steps.title: Compute block offset bits
steps.formula_raw: block size (bytes) = 2^4 words x 2^2 bytes/word = 2^6 bytes => offset = 6
steps.apply: Block = 16 words x 4 bytes = 64 bytes = 2^6 bytes. Offset bits = 6.
steps.note: This is fixed regardless of N, M, K.

steps.title: Compute set index bits
steps.formula_raw: sets = (cache_size / block_size) / K = 2^(M-6) / K => index = M - 6 - log_2(K)
steps.apply: Total lines = 2^M / 2^6 = 2^(M-6). Sets = 2^(M-6) / K = 2^(M-6-log_2 K). Index bits = M - 6 - log_2(K).
steps.note: More associativity (larger K) means fewer sets and fewer index bits.

steps.title: Compute tag bits
steps.formula_raw: tag = N - index - offset = N - (M - 6 - log_2 K) - 6 = N - M + log_2(K)
steps.apply: tag = N - (M - 6 - log_2 K) - 6 = N - M + 6 + log_2 K - 6 = N - M + log_2(K).
steps.note: The +6 and -6 cancel, leaving tag = N - M + log_2(K).

solution.result: The length of the tag field = N - M + log_2(K) bits. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Set-associative cache tag bits formula: tag = N - M + log_2(K), where N = address bits, M = log2(cache_size_bytes), K = associativity; derived from tag = N - (M - 6 - log2(K)) - 6.

questions.question:
A certain processor uses a fully associative cache of size 16 kB. The cache block size is 16 bytes. Assume that the main memory is byte addressable and uses a 32-bit address. How many bits are required for the Tag and the Index fields respectively in the addresses generated by the processor?
A. 24 bits and 0 bits
B. 24 bits and 8 bits
C. 28 bits and 0 bits
D. 28 bits and 4 bits
questions.to_find: Number of tag bits and index bits for a fully associative cache.

understand.plain: In a fully associative cache, any memory block can go into any cache line — there is no indexing. So there are zero index bits. The address is split into just tag and block offset. We need to find how many bits go to each.

keywords.explain: Every memory block can be placed in any cache line. There is no index field — the cache controller searches all lines for a tag match.
keywords.example: A 4-line fully associative cache: all 4 lines are searched on every access.

keywords.explain: In a fully associative cache, there is no fixed mapping, so index bits = 0.
keywords.example: Contrast with direct-mapped: 512 lines => 9 index bits.

keywords.explain: The lower address bits that select a byte within the block.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='420' height='120'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='30' width='300' height='50' class='hi'/><rect x='310' y='30' width='100' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='160' y='60' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Tag (28 bits)</text><text x='360' y='60' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>Offset (4 bits)</text><text x='210' y='20' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>32-bit Address (Fully Associative: Index = 0)</text><text x='10' y='100' class='sub mut'>bit 31</text><text x='390' y='100' class='sub mut'>bit 0</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 32-bit address with 28-bit tag and 4-bit block offset. No index field exists in a fully associative cache.

given.aim: Find tag bits and index bits for a fully associative cache with 16 kB capacity, 16-byte blocks, and 32-bit addresses.

terms.meaning: No index field — any block can map to any cache line.
terms.example: index bits = 0 always for fully associative.

terms.meaning: Total address space is 32 bits; number of cache lines = 16 kB / 16 B = 1024.
terms.example: 1024 lines in cache, but since fully associative, no index is needed.

given.plan: For fully associative cache: index bits = 0. Offset bits = log_2(block size). Tag bits = total address bits - offset bits - index bits.

steps.title: Determine index bits
steps.apply: Fully associative cache has no set/index structure. Index bits = 0.
steps.note: This is the defining property of fully associative caches — all lines are searched.

steps.title: Determine offset bits
steps.formula_raw: offset bits = log_2(block size in bytes)
steps.note: 4 bits can address any of 16 byte positions within a block.

steps.title: Determine tag bits
steps.formula_raw: tag bits = total address bits - index bits - offset bits
steps.note: The tag uniquely identifies which memory block is in the cache.

solution.result: Tag field = 28 bits, Index field = 0 bits. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Fully associative cache address: tag bits = total_address_bits - log2(block_size_bytes); index bits = 0 because any block can occupy any line.

questions.question: A certain processor deploys a single-level cache. The cache block size is 8 words and the word size is 4 bytes. The memory system uses a 200-MHz clock. To service a cache miss, the memory controller first takes 1 cycle to accept the starting address of the block, it then takes 8 cycles to fetch all the eight words of the block from the main memory, and finally transfers the words of the requested block at the rate of 1 word per cycle. The maximum bandwidth for the memory system when the processor is running a program that has 10% instruction cache misses and no data cache misses is _______ x 10^6 bytes/sec.
questions.to_find: Maximum memory bandwidth in units of 10^6 bytes/sec when there are 10% instruction cache misses.

understand.plain: The processor fetches instructions. 10% of instruction fetches miss the cache, requiring a memory block fetch. On a miss, the memory controller takes 1 + 8 + 8 = 17 cycles total to transfer an 8-word block (32 bytes). We need to find the average memory bandwidth (bytes transferred per second).

keywords.explain: The number of extra clock cycles needed to fetch a block from main memory when it is not in the cache.
keywords.example: If miss penalty = 17 cycles at 200 MHz, each miss takes 17/200M seconds.

keywords.explain: The rate at which data is transferred between main memory and cache, measured in bytes per second.
keywords.example: Transferring 32 bytes every 17 cycles at 200 MHz gives 32 x 200M / 17 bytes/sec.

keywords.explain: The fraction of cache accesses that result in a miss.
keywords.example: 10% miss rate means 1 in 10 accesses goes to main memory.

understand.visual_svg:
<svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='140'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='30' width='60' height='40' class='hi'/><rect x='70' y='30' width='160' height='40' class='bg'/><rect x='230' y='30' width='280' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='55' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Addr
1 cyc</text><text x='150' y='55' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Fetch 8 words
8 cycles</text><text x='370' y='55' class='sub ink' text-anchor='middle'>Transfer 8 words to cache
8 cycles (1 word/cycle)</text><text x='260' y='20' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Cache Miss Service: 1 + 8 + 8 = 17 cycles total</text><text x='40' y='110' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>1 cyc</text><text x='150' y='110' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>8 cyc</text><text x='370' y='110' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>8 cyc</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Timeline of cache miss service: 1 cycle for address acceptance, 8 cycles for memory fetch, 8 cycles for word transfer = 17 total cycles to bring 32 bytes.

given.aim: Calculate the maximum memory bandwidth with 10% instruction cache miss rate and a 200 MHz clock.

terms.term: Cache block = 8 words x 4 bytes = 32 bytes
terms.meaning: Each miss causes 32 bytes to be transferred from main memory to cache.
terms.example: 8 words at 4 bytes each = 32 bytes per block.
terms.connects: On every cache miss, 32 bytes flow through the memory bus.

terms.meaning: Address accept (1) + fetch from memory (8) + transfer to cache (8).
terms.connects: Time per miss = 17 cycles / 200 MHz = 85 ns.

terms.meaning: Every 10 instruction fetches, 1 results in a cache miss causing a block transfer.
terms.example: For every 10 instructions fetched, 32 bytes are transferred from memory once.

given.plan: Each instruction fetch = 1 cycle. With 10% miss rate, on average 1 miss per 10 instruction accesses. Each miss transfers 32 bytes in 17 cycles. Average bandwidth = (32 bytes x 0.1 miss/access) x (200 x 10^6 accesses/sec) = 32 x 0.1 x 200M = 640 x 10^6 bytes/sec. But the question asks for maximum bandwidth. On a miss, the memory transfer rate during the transfer phase is 1 word/cycle = 4 bytes/cycle = 4 x 200M = 800 x 10^6 bytes/sec.

steps.title: Identify block size and transfer rate
steps.apply: Block = 8 words x 4 bytes = 32 bytes. Transfer rate during the transfer phase = 1 word/cycle = 4 bytes/cycle.
steps.note: The transfer phase is when bandwidth is maximized.

steps.title: Calculate peak (maximum) memory bandwidth
steps.apply: Max bandwidth = 4 bytes/cycle x 200 x 10^6 cycles/sec = 800 x 10^6 bytes/sec.
steps.note: This is the maximum instantaneous bandwidth during the word transfer phase of a cache miss (1 word per cycle = 4 bytes per cycle at 200 MHz).

steps.title: Verify with miss-rate-adjusted average if needed
steps.formula_raw: avg bandwidth = (block_size x miss_rate x clock) / cycles_per_instruction
steps.apply: For 10% miss rate: avg = 32 x 0.1 x 200M = 640 MB/s. The maximum (peak) = 800 MB/s. The GATE answer is 800 (x 10^6 bytes/sec).
steps.note: The question asks for 'maximum bandwidth' which refers to the peak transfer rate of 1 word/cycle x 200 MHz = 800 MB/s.

solution.result: The maximum bandwidth is 800 x 10^6 bytes/sec.

questions.formula_note: Memory bandwidth = transfer_rate_per_cycle x clock_frequency; for cache miss with 1 word/cycle transfer at 200 MHz and 4-byte words: 4 x 200M = 800 MB/s.
